title: From Highways To Health Care: Portability Proves Key To Physical AI
gist: Physical AI progress across autos, humanoid robots, and medical devices is being driven by reusing a common technology base -- lithography/chiplet techniques, hyperspectral imaging sensors, and AI-on-raw-data models -- with imec's automotive chiplet program now being explicitly ported into a broader edge/robotics chiplet standard.

themes.theme: Reticle stitching as an alternative to chiplet splitting

insights.claim: The lithography exposure field is capped at 26 x 33 mm by reticle size, which limits how large a single chip/SoC can be and is a core reason the industry split SoCs into chiplets.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Explains a root physical constraint behind the entire chiplet trend, useful for judging how durable that trend is.
insights.locator: Increasing die size drives reticle stitching solutions

insights.claim: As a competing approach to chiplets, engineers are now stitching two adjacent reticle fields together to form one larger chip, which moves the distortion-prone field edge from the die's boundary into the middle of the design, requiring active correction.
insights.speaker: Germain Fenger
insights.so_what: A new patterning technique that could let chipmakers build larger monolithic dies again despite reticle-size limits, competing with the chiplet approach for certain use cases.
insights.quote: The field edge introduces significant distortion, which affects imaging
insights.locator: Germain Fenger, Synopsys, on reticle stitching

insights.claim: Leading chipmakers already combine inverse lithography technology (ILT) and optical proximity correction (OPC) at the most critical exposure layers at 5nm and below, and this same combination -- plus locally modified design rules -- is what enables reticle stitching to work.
insights.speaker: Germain Fenger
insights.so_what: Shows stitching is not a brand-new toolchain but a repurposing of existing advanced-node correction methods, lowering the barrier to adoption.
insights.locator: Germain Fenger, Synopsys, on ILT/OPC combination

insights.claim: Rivian's first-generation in-house autonomy processor, RAP1, is a custom 5nm SoC built by TSMC and integrated with three HBM memory stacks.
insights.speaker: Vidya Rajagopalan
insights.so_what: Signals automakers now designing leading-edge, HBM-equipped silicon in-house rather than buying off-the-shelf chips.
insights.locator: Automotive through vertical integration

insights.claim: By 2030, roughly 50% of a car's bill of materials will be electronics, reflecting the growing software complexity of modern vehicles.
insights.speaker: Vidya Rajagopalan
insights.so_what: A concrete forward estimate investors can use to size the semiconductor content opportunity in vehicles.
insights.quote: By 2030, about 50% of the car’s bill of materials will be electronics
insights.locator: Vidya Rajagopalan, Rivian, on 2030 vehicle BOM

insights.claim: Rivian took a clean-sheet, vertically integrated approach, treating the vehicle as one large network computer instead of the traditional model where OEMs simply integrated units from many specialized Tier One suppliers, which previously resulted in vehicles containing 40 to 100 separate electronic control units (ECUs).
insights.speaker: Vidya Rajagopalan
insights.so_what: Vertical integration can collapse dozens of ECUs into a small number of PCBs, a structural cost and complexity advantage that most legacy OEMs cannot easily replicate.
insights.locator: Using co-development, but kicking it up a notch

insights.claim: Only two companies in the Western hemisphere, plus a handful in China, have taken this fully vertically integrated, clean-sheet approach to vehicle electronics design.
insights.speaker: Vidya Rajagopalan
insights.so_what: Suggests a narrow competitive set currently capable of this architecture, a potential moat versus legacy OEMs still using Tier One-integrated designs.
insights.locator: Rivian vertical integration discussion

insights.claim: Automotive packaging is expected to become far more sophisticated, with much more complex multichip modules combining memory, SoCs, and power management, since determinism and low latency -- talked about broadly for physical AI -- are already assumed requirements in self-driving cars.
insights.speaker: Vidya Rajagopalan
insights.so_what: Points to rising packaging complexity/cost in next-gen auto silicon, relevant to packaging and OSAT suppliers.
insights.locator: Rivian third-generation vehicle discussion

insights.claim: At advanced nodes, chip performance is no longer set by any single process step but by the interaction between processes such as bonding, thinning, CMP, and packaging, which is why materials are now validated across full process flows rather than in isolation.
insights.speaker: Douglas Guerrero
insights.so_what: A framework for why materials suppliers now need fab-level co-development partnerships (like imec) rather than solo R&D.
insights.quote: At advanced nodes, performance is no longer driven by single steps, but by the interaction between processes such as bonding, thinning, CMP, and packaging
insights.locator: Douglas Guerrero, Brewer Science

insights.claim: A Brewer Science/imec collaboration produced a commercial-ready material platform meeting tighter thermal stability, adhesion, and ultraclean-debond specs with less process variability, and Brewer's own technologist says the project likely would have taken longer without imec's integrated process flows and cross-industry feedback loops.
insights.speaker: Douglas Guerrero
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence that shared R&D consortia like imec measurably speed time-to-market for advanced materials.
insights.locator: Brewer Science / imec material platform example

themes.theme: Hyperspectral imaging: one sensor platform, many markets

insights.claim: Optical filters deposited directly on standard CMOS image sensors enable hyperspectral imaging, and this single underlying technology is being adapted to preserve healthy tissue during surgery, check food freshness, optimize crop irrigation, detect blood or gunpowder at crime scenes, and sort plastics for recycling.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Illustrates the article's core portability thesis: one sensor platform amortized across many disparate end markets, improving unit economics for chipmakers.
insights.locator: One base technology, dozens of applications

insights.claim: The hyperspectral filters are built from many flat thin layers aligned with lithographic precision to individual pixels at roughly a 5-micrometer pitch, using a wet cavity etch to create a staircase over the pixel array where each step's cavity thickness sets that pixel's central filter wavelength.
insights.speaker: Tina Babu Shylaja
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete manufacturing mechanism for how compact, low-cost hyperspectral cameras are actually built at pixel scale.
insights.locator: Tina Babu Shylaja, imec, on filter fabrication

insights.claim: An FDA-cleared hyperspectral imaging device for open and minimally invasive surgery detects oxygen saturation in tissue to flag ischemia risk, helping surgeons decide precisely where to cut so the remaining tissue has enough blood flow to heal.
insights.speaker: Josh Gibson
insights.so_what: A concrete, already-commercialized example of hyperspectral imaging changing surgical decision-making, not just a lab concept.
insights.locator: Josh Gibson, Hypervision Surgical

insights.claim: Hyperspectral imaging of the retina is being explored as a way to non-invasively detect the signatures of Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, and Huntington's diseases before symptoms appear, whereas these conditions are typically diagnosed only after onset today.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: An early-stage but high-value diagnostic application that could shift neurodegenerative disease detection years earlier.
insights.locator: Retina hyperspectral imaging discussion

themes.theme: AI unlocks data medicine has been throwing away

insights.claim: Most medical measurements undergo lossy waveform compression tuned to human perception, which discards medically relevant information -- and ML/transformer models trained directly on raw waveforms like EKGs are now revealing signals no clinician or cardiologist can see, such as detecting atrial fibrillation from a decade earlier or structural heart defects normally only visible on ultrasound.
insights.speaker: Dan Wattendorf
insights.so_what: Suggests huge untapped diagnostic value sitting in already-collected but compressed medical data, a potential new market for AI-driven diagnostics.
insights.quote: we’re starting to see astonishing things that no clinician or cardiologist can see
insights.locator: Dan Wattendorf, Gates Foundation, on EKG AI

insights.claim: A complete blood count (CBC) test compresses about 9 million underlying data points down to roughly 20 numbers that a physician actually sees, illustrating how much raw physiological signal is discarded in standard clinical reporting.
insights.speaker: Dan Wattendorf
insights.so_what: A striking, quantified example of the compression problem that AI could reverse to extract more diagnostic value from routine tests.
insights.locator: Dan Wattendorf, Gates Foundation, on CBC compression

insights.claim: A Mass General Brigham study tracking 12,000 healthy patients over 20 years found that individuals' data fall within very different personal distributions and setpoints, meaning a result within normal population-level specification can still miss a strong deviation from an individual's own baseline.
insights.speaker: Dan Wattendorf
insights.so_what: Argues for personalized baselines over population norms in diagnostics -- a framework shift that AI-scale data analysis could enable.
insights.locator: Mass General Brigham 12,000-patient study

insights.claim: Developers of humanoid robots say they cannot afford to rewrite the compute stack for each new robot, so determinism, openness, and scalability -- not peak TOPS -- are the actual driving forces behind physical AI hardware architecture decisions.
insights.speaker: Salil Raje
insights.so_what: Reframes the competitive metric for robotics silicon away from raw compute (TOPS) toward reusability and predictability, which should shift how buyers evaluate chips.
insights.quote: Determinism, openness, and scalability are the driving forces behind physical AI architectures, not peak TOPS
insights.locator: Salil Raje, AMD, on physical AI architecture

insights.claim: A humanoid robot's compute architecture splits into a 'brain' running perception, planning, and LLMs; a synchronous time-sensitive-network 'spine' with one global clock aligning all nodes to microsecond latencies; and a 'body' of hundreds of sensors and limb/joint/motor controllers handling safety moves, torque, and actuation locally.
insights.speaker: Salil Raje
insights.so_what: A reusable mental model for how robotics compute is being architected, useful for evaluating which vendors' chips fit which layer.
insights.locator: Salil Raje, AMD, on brain/spine/body architecture

insights.claim: To meet real-time response requirements, robots encode, filter, and resize sensor data locally and send only insights, tokens, and metadata to the central brain, which achieves a two-to-three orders of magnitude reduction in data traffic.
insights.speaker: Salil Raje
insights.so_what: Quantifies why edge preprocessing is essential for physical AI -- a 100-1000x bandwidth reduction changes network and compute design requirements dramatically.
insights.quote: you send only insights, tokens, metadata to the central brain, and that enables a two to three orders of magnitude of improvement in data traffic
insights.locator: Salil Raje, AMD, on edge data reduction

themes.theme: Power efficiency as the binding constraint on physical AI

insights.claim: The human brain's computational efficiency is roughly 10,000 times greater than anything current electronic devices have produced, and bio-inspired 'sensory language models' (SLMs) rather than LLMs are being explored to close that efficiency gap for edge AI.
insights.speaker: Ahmed Bahai
insights.so_what: Frames energy efficiency, not raw model capability, as the key gating factor for physical AI going mainstream.
insights.quote: the efficiency of our brains is about 10,000 times greater than anything we have produced today
insights.locator: Ahmed Bahai, Texas Instruments, on brain efficiency

insights.claim: Today's edge devices are more power-limited than transistor-limited, meaning energy efficiency -- not further transistor scaling -- will be the deciding factor in whether generative AI and physical AI gain broad public acceptance.
insights.speaker: Ahmed Bahai
insights.so_what: A contrarian framing against the usual Moore's-Law/transistor-count narrative, pointing investors toward power-efficiency innovation as the real bottleneck.
insights.locator: Ahmed Bahai, Texas Instruments, on power vs. transistor limits

themes.theme: Portability from auto chiplets to a broader edge/robotics standard

insights.claim: imec's Automotive Chiplet Program (ACP), launched in 2024, has assembled 24 members spanning OEMs, Tier 1 suppliers, IC manufacturers, EDA companies, and OSATs, and helped catalyze the Bosch-led European CHASSIS project for chiplet-based software-defined vehicle architectures.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: Shows a maturing, well-populated industry consortium already standardizing automotive chiplets, the base imec is now generalizing to other markets.
insights.locator: Progress from automotive informs everything autonomous

insights.claim: imec renamed and broadened its automotive effort into the Autonomous Edge Chiplet Program (AECP), explicitly porting the ACP's architectural learnings to humanoid robots, drones, and industrial automation, because there is common architectural DNA -- compute, AI, safety, memory, scalability -- across chiplets in different verticals.
insights.speaker: Dieter Hoffend
insights.so_what: Directly evidences the article's core portability thesis at the industry-consortium level: automotive chiplet standards becoming the template for robotics and industrial edge AI.
insights.quote: There is a common architectural DNA within chiplets that scales across multiple industries
insights.locator: Dieter Hoffend, imec, on AECP

insights.claim: imec opened a new chiplet acceleration center in Heilbronn, Germany, focused specifically on prototyping and de-risking chiplet adoption for the automotive industry by bridging research and industrialization.
insights.speaker: Dieter Hoffend
insights.so_what: A concrete physical investment signaling imec is moving chiplet work from research toward production-ready prototyping infrastructure.
insights.locator: Dieter Hoffend, imec, on Heilbronn site

insights.claim: While 96.4 million vehicles were manufactured in 2025, automotive volumes stay relatively flat year to year compared to consumer electronics, making automotive a comparatively small semiconductor market even as compute demand per vehicle grows exponentially -- a mismatch imec believes chiplets are the answer to.
insights.speaker: Dieter Hoffend
insights.so_what: Frames chiplets as the economic mechanism needed to reconcile exponential compute demand against a market too small to fund custom monolithic silicon at scale -- a lens applicable to other slow-volume-growth verticals adopting AI.
insights.quote: So how do we reconcile exponential compute demand with linear market economies? We at imec believe chiplets are the answer.
insights.locator: Dieter Hoffend, imec, on market economics

insights.claim: The AECP has set a 12-month deadline to build a reference platform for edge applications prioritizing interoperability among chiplets from multiple vendors and hardware/software co-design, and imec is working with Japan's Advanced SoC Research for Automotive (ASRA) to harmonize chiplet architecture standards internationally.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: A concrete near-term milestone and a sign the chiplet standardization effort is going global, not staying EU-only.
insights.locator: AECP 12-month deadline and ASRA collaboration
